items.title: Every GL posting must balance debits to credits
items.description: All posting to the general ledger must satisfy sum(debit) == sum(credit) within currency tolerance. The balance invariant is enforced in one domain method that every document posts through, so no module can write an unbalanced set of GL entries.
items.applicability: Posting from any source document — journal, invoice, payment, stock or production voucher.
items.example: A sales invoice posts debit Receivable and credit Income plus Tax; the totals match before any GL row is written, or the post is rejected.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: centralise the balance check so it cannot be bypassed per module. Test implication: an unbalanced posting attempt is rejected for every document type. Generator implication: the posting engine template carries the single balance method. Boundary: balance enforces arithmetic integrity, not correct account selection.

items.title: Post documents through a maker/checker workflow with approval thresholds
items.description: Documents move through a maker/checker workflow: a maker submits, a checker approves or rejects, and configurable thresholds decide when a second approval is required. The maker and the checker must be different users so no one approves their own document.
items.applicability: Any document whose posting has financial or inventory effect.
items.example: A payment over the threshold needs a checker other than the maker to approve before it posts; under the threshold it can auto-route per policy.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce maker != checker and threshold rules in the domain, not the UI. Test implication: a self-approval and a below-threshold-without-approval attempt are both rejected. Generator implication: the workflow template drives every postable document. Boundary: maker/checker controls authorisation flow, not the business correctness of the document content.

items.title: Require a reason on every rejection
items.description: When a checker rejects a document, a non-empty rejection reason is mandatory and is recorded with the rejection event. A rejection with no reason is not permitted, so the audit trail always explains why a document was sent back.
items.applicability: The reject path of every maker/checker workflow.
items.example: Rejecting a purchase invoice without text is blocked; supplying 'price mismatch with PO' records the reason against the event.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: validate the reason at the domain boundary and persist it with the event. Test implication: a reject with an empty reason is rejected and a reject with a reason is recorded. Generator implication: the workflow template wires the required-reason field. Boundary: a required reason guarantees an explanation exists, not that the explanation is accurate.

items.title: Keep an append-only audit trail
items.description: Document and security events are written to an append-only audit trail. Audit rows are never updated or deleted in normal operation, so the history of what happened, by whom and when, is preserved intact.
items.applicability: Submission, approval, rejection, cancellation and security events.
items.example: Submitting then cancelling a document leaves two audit rows; neither is mutated, and the sequence reads as the true history.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: model audit as insert-only with no update/delete code path. Test implication: an attempt to mutate an audit row has no supported path and history survives a document's lifecycle. Generator implication: the audit template is insert-only. Boundary: append-only preserves history; it does not by itself prevent a privileged out-of-band database edit.

items.title: Soft-delete masters but keep posted documents immutable
items.description: Master records (items, parties, accounts) are soft-deleted so references stay intact, while posted documents are immutable: they move through submit, approve, cancel and reverse, and are never hard-deleted or edited in place. Correcting a posted document means a reversal, not an overwrite.
items.applicability: Deletion and correction of masters versus transactions.
items.example: An item master is soft-deleted and hidden, while a posted invoice is corrected by a reversal entry rather than being edited or removed.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: gate masters with an IsDeleted flag and forbid hard-delete/edit on posted documents. Test implication: hard-deleting a posted document and editing a submitted one are both rejected. Generator implication: lifecycle templates differ for masters versus documents. Boundary: immutability protects the ledger, at the cost of more reversal entries for corrections.

items.title: Value stock with moving-average against a stock ledger
items.description: Inventory is valued on a moving-average basis, recomputed on each receipt, and every movement writes a stock-ledger entry. Valuation and on-hand quantity are derived from the stock ledger, not from a mutable per-item number kept in isolation.
items.applicability: Receipts, issues, transfers and consumption of stock items.
items.example: Receiving 10 at 100 then 10 at 120 sets a moving average of 110; issuing 5 values the issue at 110 and writes a ledger row.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: post a stock-ledger entry per movement and recompute the average atomically. Test implication: a mixed-cost receipt-and-issue sequence yields the expected average and ledger rows. Generator implication: the stock template emits the ledger and valuation logic. Boundary: moving-average is one valuation method; FIFO or standard costing would need a different ledger treatment.

items.title: Drive production from a BOM with issue, quality gate and finished-goods receipt at computed cost
items.description: Manufacturing runs from a bill of materials: components are issued to a work order, a quality gate must pass, and finished goods are received at a unit cost computed from issued material and applied costs. The flow ties material consumption to output valuation.
items.applicability: Work orders that convert components into finished goods.
items.example: A work order issues components worth 1000 and applies 200 of conversion; passing quality, it receives finished goods at the computed per-unit cost.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: gate finished-goods receipt on the quality step and compute unit cost from actual issues. Test implication: a run that skips the quality gate cannot receive finished goods, and the received cost matches the computed figure. Generator implication: the manufacturing template wires issue, quality and receipt. Boundary: this models discrete BOM production, not process or co-product manufacturing.

items.title: Hash passwords with PBKDF2 and enforce lockout and a password policy
items.description: Authentication stores passwords as PBKDF2 hashes with a per-user salt, enforces an account lockout after repeated failures, and applies a password policy. Plaintext or weak hashing is never used, and lockout blunts brute-force attempts.
items.example: Five failed logins lock the account for the configured window; stored credentials are PBKDF2 hashes that fail a policy-violating password at set time.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: use PBKDF2 with a sufficient iteration count and a unique salt, plus a lockout counter. Test implication: lockout triggers after the threshold and a weak password is rejected. Generator implication: the auth template emits hashing, lockout and policy. Boundary: PBKDF2 with lockout is a sound baseline, not a substitute for an external security review.

items.title: Apply anti-forgery protection and audit security events
items.description: State-changing requests carry anti-forgery protection, and security events — logins, failures, lockouts, permission denials — are written to the append-only audit trail. The application can show who did what and when across the security surface.
items.example: A POST without a valid anti-forgery token is rejected, and a failed login plus the resulting lockout are both audited.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: require the anti-forgery token on state-changing endpoints and emit a security audit row per event. Test implication: a tokenless POST is rejected and a failed login produces an audit record. Generator implication: the auth template wires anti-forgery and security auditing. Boundary: these controls cover common web threats, not the full scope of an external penetration test.

items.title: Commit EF migrations with a design-time factory
items.description: Schema is managed through committed EF Core migrations, and a design-time DbContext factory is provided so tooling can build the context without running the app. Migrations are the schema source of truth and are kept consistent with the model snapshot.
items.applicability: Any schema change in the generated or reference application.
items.example: Adding a column produces a committed migration; the design-time factory lets 'dotnet ef' generate it without launching the web host.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: include a design-time factory and regenerate the snapshot through EF, never by hand. Test implication: migrations apply cleanly to an empty database and the snapshot matches the model. Generator implication: the data template emits the factory and migration scaffolding. Boundary: committed migrations match the deterministic model; non-deterministic additions are not in the committed migration.

items.title: Migrate() on SQL Server, EnsureCreated() on portable SQLite
items.description: On the SQL Server target the application applies committed migrations with Migrate(); on a portable SQLite target used for tests or demos it uses EnsureCreated() to build the schema from the model. The two paths are chosen by provider so each target gets the right bootstrap.
items.applicability: Database initialisation across the primary server and a portable test database.
items.example: Startup against SQL Server runs Migrate() to apply the committed history, while the SQLite test database is created directly with EnsureCreated().
items.limitation: Implementation implication: branch initialisation on the active provider, not on a single hard-coded call. Test implication: the SQL Server path applies migrations and the SQLite path creates the schema, both yielding a usable database. Generator implication: the startup template selects the path by provider. Boundary: EnsureCreated() bypasses migration history, so the portable database is for tests and demos, not migration validation.

items.title: Prove a live SQL Server migration and login against LocalDB
items.description: Each sprint proves the application against a live SQL Server (LocalDB) by applying the committed migrations to a real instance and completing a login, not just against the portable SQLite test database. This catches provider-specific schema and auth issues that SQLite would mask.
items.applicability: Verification that the primary database path works end to end.
items.example: A verification run points the connection string at (localdb)\\MSSQLLocalDB, applies migrations, seeds, and logs in a user, proving the SQL Server path before handover.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: include a live-LocalDB migrate-and-login check in the verification set distinct from the SQLite tests. Test implication: migrations apply to LocalDB and a login succeeds against it. Generator implication: the verification template targets the real provider, not only the portable one. Boundary: LocalDB proves the SQL Server path locally, not behaviour against a remote production server or its TLS and auth configuration.

items.title: Generate generic CRUD UI by reflection with stable data-testid hooks
items.description: A reflection-driven generic CRUD UI renders list, create, edit and detail views from entity metadata, and every interactive element carries a stable data-testid. The stable hooks let Playwright target controls without depending on fragile text or layout.
items.applicability: Master and document maintenance screens generated across modules.
items.example: The generic editor renders an item form from its metadata and tags the save button data-testid='item-save', which a Playwright test clicks reliably.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive fields by reflection and assign deterministic data-testid values. Test implication: a Playwright run drives create/edit via the stable hooks without selector churn. Generator implication: the UI template emits the reflective renderer and the hooks. Boundary: reflection-driven UI covers standard CRUD; bespoke screens still need hand-authored views.

items.title: Scope reports to a company and reconcile them to the GL
items.description: Reports are scoped to a company and must reconcile to the general ledger: a trial balance, P&L or balance sheet derives from the same GL entries, so the report totals tie back to the ledger rather than to a separate, drifting calculation.
items.applicability: Financial and subledger reporting across one or more companies.
items.example: The company trial balance sums the same GL entries as the ledger view, so the two always agree for that company.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: build reports off the GL with a company filter, not off a parallel aggregate. Test implication: a report's totals reconcile to the GL for the scoped company. Generator implication: the report template reads the GL and applies company scope. Boundary: GL reconciliation guarantees internal consistency, not conformance to any specific external reporting standard.

items.title: Give every business rule a negative test
items.description: Each enforced business rule is covered by a negative test that proves the rule actually blocks the disallowed case: RBAC-negative for unauthorised access, maker-checker-negative for self-approval, and insufficient-stock for over-issue. A rule with only happy-path coverage is treated as unproven.
items.applicability: Test coverage for every control in the application.
items.example: Tests assert an unauthorised user is denied, a maker cannot approve their own document, and an issue exceeding on-hand stock is rejected.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: pair each rule with an explicit failing-case test, not just a success case. Test implication: removing a guard makes its negative test fail. Generator implication: the test template emits negative cases alongside positive ones. Boundary: negative tests prove the specific disallowed cases written; they do not exhaustively cover every possible violation.
